name: openness
description: willingness to engage with novel ideas, perspectives, and situations. curiosity about the world, appreciation for diverse viewpoints, and comfort with exploration beyond familiar boundaries.
howToPractice: seek out a perspective this week that genuinely challenges your own. sit with it long enough to find what’s useful in it.

name: adaptability
description: capacity to adjust thoughts, behaviors, and strategies in response to changing conditions. cognitive flexibility, tolerance for uncertainty, and willingness to revise plans when circumstances shift.
howToPractice: next time a plan falls apart, notice your first reaction. then ask: what does this new situation make possible that the old plan didn’t?

name: agency
description: sense that one can influence outcomes through intentional action. self-efficacy, internal locus of control, and belief in capacity to shape learning and work.
howToPractice: identify one thing you’ve been waiting for permission to do. take the smallest possible step toward it today.

name: curiosity
description: intrinsic drive to seek new information and experiences. asking questions, exploring unknowns, finding joy in discovery.
howToPractice: ask three genuine questions today where you don’t already know the answer. notice which ones surprise you.

name: intellectual humility
description: recognition that one’s beliefs and knowledge are fallible. openness to being wrong, respect for others’ viewpoints, and willingness to revise judgments based on evidence.
howToPractice: in your next disagreement, try saying ‘i might be wrong about this — help me see what i’m missing.’ notice what shifts.

name: willingness to take intellectual risks
description: willingness to try new approaches, share tentative ideas, and persist despite uncertainty. risk-taking supports creative thinking and deep learning.
howToPractice: share an unfinished idea in your next meeting. frame it as ‘this is rough, but...’ and see what develops.

name: tolerance for ambiguity
description: ability to function effectively in situations lacking clear structure, answers, or outcomes. comfort with complexity, patience with not-knowing, capacity to hold multiple possibilities simultaneously.
howToPractice: next time you feel the urge to resolve something prematurely, try sitting with the uncertainty for one more day. notice what clarity emerges on its own.

name: perspective-taking & empathic concern
description: cognitive capacity to understand another person’s viewpoint combined with emotional response to others’ experiences. enables consideration of stakeholder impacts, navigation of difference, and compassionate action.
howToPractice: before reacting to someone’s behavior, spend 30 seconds imagining three possible reasons behind it that have nothing to do with you.

name: self-regulation
description: monitoring and managing one’s emotions, attention, and behaviors to achieve goals. impulse control, emotional awareness, stress management, and strategic resource allocation.
howToPractice: when you feel a spike of reactivity, put one hand on your chest and take three slow breaths. not to suppress the feeling — to expand the space around it.

name: abundance mindset
description: belief that opportunities, resources, and success are plentiful rather than zero-sum. generosity, collaboration over competition, and confidence that enough exists for all.
howToPractice: next time you feel competitive or guarded, ask: what would i do differently if i believed there was enough for everyone? try that.

name: inquiry
description: asking questions, seeking evidence, and investigating phenomena with systematic curiosity. formulating meaningful questions, designing investigations, and pursuing answers through research or experimentation.
howToPractice: take a claim you’ve accepted at face value. spend 10 minutes investigating it. notice whether the evidence matches your assumption.

name: analysis & evaluation
description: breaking down complex information into components to understand relationships, patterns, and structures. judging quality, validity, or significance based on criteria.
howToPractice: read an argument you agree with and identify its weakest point. read one you disagree with and find its strongest.

name: synthesis
description: integration of ideas from multiple sources into coherent new understanding. identifying connections, building frameworks, and creating unified perspectives from diverse inputs.
howToPractice: after reading or learning from two different sources on a topic, write one paragraph that connects them in a way neither source does alone.

name: extending ideas
description: taking existing concepts or solutions and pushing them further. applying them to new contexts, elaborating their implications, and building upon them incrementally.
howToPractice: take a solution that works in one domain and ask: where else could this apply? what would need to change to make it work there?

name: conventional ideas
description: common, established, or standard approaches within a domain. provides necessary grounding — you must know the norms to meaningfully innovate beyond them.
howToPractice: before trying to innovate in an area, spend time understanding the established approach. ask: why does this convention exist? what problem did it solve?

name: generating diverse ideas
description: producing many different kinds of ideas across categories, domains, or perspectives. emphasis on breadth and variety over depth or refinement.
howToPractice: set a timer for 5 minutes and generate ideas for a current challenge. aim for quantity and variety — no filtering allowed until the timer ends.

name: generating creative ideas
description: producing original, novel, or unusual ideas that break from conventional patterns. divergent thinking, remote association, and willingness to propose unexpected possibilities.
howToPractice: combine two unrelated concepts from your work and ask: what would this look like if they merged? follow the weird ones.

name: improving ideas
description: taking initial concepts and refining, elaborating, or enhancing them through iteration. giving and receiving feedback, recognizing limitations, and making strategic revisions.
howToPractice: take your most recent piece of work and ask three people for one specific improvement each. implement the one that surprises you most.

name: planning
description: setting goals, anticipating steps, allocating resources, and organizing action toward desired outcomes. strategic thinking, time management, and breaking complex goals into manageable parts.
howToPractice: take your biggest current goal and break it into three phases. for each phase, identify the single most important next action.

name: executing
description: implementation of plans. taking action, managing resources, coordinating efforts, and maintaining momentum toward goals. involves persistence, task management, and adaptive implementation when plans meet reality.
howToPractice: identify the task you’ve been avoiding longest. commit to 15 minutes on it right now — nothing else. momentum follows motion.

name: monitoring
description: tracking progress toward goals, assessing whether strategies are working, and recognizing when adjustments are needed. metacognitive awareness, self-assessment, and use of feedback.
howToPractice: at the end of today, ask: what worked? what didn’t? what will i do differently tomorrow? write it down in three sentences.
